# llm-wiki — Compounding Vault Knowledge

## Overview

Orchestrates Karpathy-style wiki maintenance across Justin's three-layer taxonomy: immutable **Inputs**, compiled bibliographical **Sources**, and the unchanged **maturity ladder** (Notes→Thoughts→Concepts→Beliefs→References). Hybrid integration: light log append after ingest, full compile on demand, query synthesis for durable answers.

Deep reference: [architecture](references/architecture.md) | [integrate-light](references/integrate-light.md) | [integrate-entities](references/integrate-entities.md) | [integrate-full](references/integrate-full.md) | [integrate-query](references/integrate-query.md) | [lint](references/lint.md) | [index-and-log](references/index-and-log.md) | [taxonomy-migration](references/taxonomy-migration.md)

## When to Use

- After explicit ingest (email dispatch, Slack brain, Readwise, Granola reconcile)
- "integrate this", "wiki integrate today", "compile today's ingests"
- Wind-down EIIRP Step 5 (integrate-full)
- Research Q&A that should persist in the vault (integrate-query): "what do my notes say about…", "compare X and Y", "synthesize everything on Z"
- "run wiki lint" (semantic; structural → obsidian-hygiene)

**Don't use for:** daily work logs (work-log), structural hygiene (obsidian-hygiene), maturity promotion (obsidian-suggest-promotions — **unchanged**).

## Vault Path

`OBSIDIAN_VAULT_PATH` from `~/.hermes/.env`. Input paths use `Inputs/` (legacy `Logs/` accepted during migration).

## Three Layers

| Layer | Folder | Category | Immutable? | Bes edits body? |
|-------|--------|----------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 Inputs | `Inputs/Readings/` etc. | `[[Readings]]`, `[[Meetings]]`, `[[Emails]]`, `[[Slack]]`, `[[Linear]]` | Yes | Metadata only |
| 2 Compiled | `Notes/` | `[[Sources]]` | No | Yes (summary) |
| 3 Maturity | `Notes/` (+ subfolders) | Notes, Thoughts, Concepts, Beliefs, References, Decisions, Memories, Projects | No | Yes |

**Layer 3 ladder is unchanged.** Promotion Notes→Thoughts→Beliefs stays in `obsidian-suggest-promotions`. llm-wiki does not auto-promote maturity tiers.

**Link direction:** Concept → Source → Reading. Never Concept → Reading when Source exists.

## Integration Passes

| Pass | When | Actions |
|------|------|---------|
| **integrate-light** | Every explicit ingest; cron post-steps | Append `Utilities/log.md` with daily note wikilink. **Never modify Input bodies.** No index or notepad updates. |
| **integrate-entities** | After integrate-light; meeting reconcile | Update existing projects' State sections on decisions/status-changes only. Timelines are disabled. Update-only — no stub creation. |
| **integrate-full** | Wind-down Step 5; manual triggers | Reading→Source promotion, project/contact cross-refs, contradiction flags |
| **integrate-query** | Interactive durable Q&A | Search → synthesize → write to `Inbox/` when durability met (3+ notes, comparison/decision analysis, cross-project connection, explicit "file this", same topic twice) → print complete note in channel, log append. See [integrate-query.md](references/integrate-query.md). Template: [query-synthesis.md](templates/query-synthesis.md). |

Cron runs **integrate-light + integrate-entities**. No auto-vault from raw streams.

## integrate-light (default after ingest)

See [integrate-light.md](references/integrate-light.md).

1. Append one line to `Utilities/log.md` (time, type, note wikilink, path, daily note wikilink)
2. Create `Utilities/log.md` from `templates/log.md` if missing (see [integrate-light.md](references/integrate-light.md))
3. Run `integrate_entities.py` on the ingest (see [integrate-entities.md](references/integrate-entities.md))
4. Confirm hub updates from JSON report; set ingest `project:` frontmatter when script matches exactly one project

**Immutability:** Do not edit bodies under `Inputs/Readings/`, `Inputs/Emails/`, `Inputs/Slack/`, `Inputs/Linear/`.

## integrate-full (on demand)

See [integrate-full.md](references/integrate-full.md).

**Manual triggers:** "integrate this", "wiki integrate today", "compile today's ingests", "integrate [[Project]]", "run wiki lint".

**Reading → Source promotion:** For each scoped Reading, create or update `Notes/<Title>.md`:

```yaml
---
id: 'YYYYMMDDHHmmss'
daily_note: "[[YYYY-MM-DD Weekday|YYYY-MM-DD Weekday]]"
category: "[[Sources]]"
---
```

```markdown
# Title

- **Author:**
- **URL:**
- **Type:** book | article | paper | video

## Summary
Bes-compiled synthesis (kept current by integrate-full).

## Raw inputs
- [[Reading note title]]

## Related
- [[Concept or Project links]]
```

Confirm with Justin before bulk mature-note edits.

## integrate-query

See [integrate-query.md](references/integrate-query.md). Search → synthesize → write to `Inbox/` for manual review when durability threshold met → print complete note in channel and run integrate-light log append. Template: [query-synthesis.md](templates/query-synthesis.md). Interactive sessions only — not cron.

## Semantic lint (tier-3)

See [lint.md](references/lint.md).

**Monthly cron** (`wiki_semantic_lint_cron.py`, 1st of month): deterministic orphans, stale summaries, promotion gaps, link chains, contradiction candidates → `Utilities/reports/semantic-lint-*.md` + `~/.hermes/state/semantic_lint_last.json`.

**On-demand:** Run `vault_hygiene.py` first (structural), then `wiki_semantic_lint.py`. Agent reviews contradiction candidates — never auto-edit Beliefs.

## Boundaries

| Skill | Role |
|-------|------|
| work-log | Daily activity — not wiki compile |
| obsidian-hygiene | ID, folders, ghosts, URLs |
| obsidian-suggest-promotions | Maturity ladder — **do not modify** |
| bes-email-dispatch | Ingest emails → then integrate-light |

## Taxonomy migration

One-time `Logs/` → `Inputs/` via `scripts/migrate_logs_to_inputs.py`. See [taxonomy-migration.md](references/taxonomy-migration.md). Category templates for `Utilities/Categories/Readings.md` and rewritten `Sources.md` included there.

## Common Pitfalls

1. Compiling into Reading bodies — Inputs are immutable; compile to Source notes only.
2. Skipping integrate-light after cron ingest — log drifts; ingests become untraceable.
3. Auto-promoting maturity tiers — belongs to obsidian-suggest-promotions, not llm-wiki.
4. Linking Concepts directly to Readings — use Source as intermediary.
5. Running integrate-full from cron without approval — light pass only in autonomous runs.
6. Generating dense, bulky notes during query synthesis — keep integrations and syntheses highly atomic and focused, avoiding oversized multi-topic summaries.
7. Attempting to write timeline or related inputs lists to contact or project notes — automated timelines are disabled in favor of native backlinks; only update project `## State` on decisions/status.
8. Neglecting Log Maintenance: Failing to append to `Utilities/log.md` on every ingest makes files untraceable and breaks Karpathy-style chronological auditing.

## Verification Checklist

- [ ] integrate-light appended log line with daily note wikilink
- [ ] integrate-entities updated matched hub pages (or report shows unmatched only)
- [ ] No Input body edits on Readings/Emails/Slack/Linear
- [ ] Compiled Sources have `## Raw inputs` with Reading links
- [ ] Layer 3 notes untouched unless integrate-full explicitly scoped
- [ ] obsidian-hygiene run for structural baseline before semantic lint
- [ ] integrate-query: filed note exists in `Inbox/` with Question, Synthesis, Sources consulted sections
- [ ] integrate-query: printed the complete created note in the chat channel for review
- [ ] integrate-query: log line appended with `query` token
- [ ] integrate-query: Sources consulted section populated with upstream wikilinks

The user has provided the following instruction alongside the skill invocation: [IMPORTANT: You are running as a scheduled cron job. DELIVERY: Your final response will be automatically delivered to the user — do NOT use send_message or try to deliver the output yourself. Just produce your report/output as your final response and the system handles the rest. SILENT: If there is genuinely nothing new to report, respond with exactly "[SILENT]" (nothing else) to suppress delivery. Never combine [SILENT] with content — either report your findings normally, or say [SILENT] and nothing more.]

          "user_id": "U095LHMC4UW",
          "user_name": "Justin",
          "text": "B2C product idea: AI-native, audio-friendly flashcard app?\n\nSpeak to create cards; speak in response (fuzzy scoring via LLM); AI-assisted card creation &amp; curation (ex, ask for cards on a topic &amp; get them magically)\n\nProblems it solves: More accessible for people with dyslexia or visual impairments. Easy to use while you're on the go.\n\nDuolingo risk: This would be less hard for them to copy than video-based stuff. But the same logic that killed Tinycards probably still applies - Duolingo has other, higher-ROI opportunities to leverage what they already have."
        },
        {
          "ts": "1781173397.754469",
          "user_id": "U095LHMC4UW",
          "user_name": "Justin",
          "text": "This makes me think there might be a general strategy here:\n\n\u2022 SignLab succeeds partly because it fills a gap Duolingo doesn't (video-based learning).\n\u2022 Duolingo _could_ fill many of these gaps, but ROI is low vs other ideas that leverage what Duolingo already has and does well.\n\u2022 The sum of the gaps likely has a smaller addressable market than Duolingo's.\n\u2022 But if we stick with a small, AI-native team, that could be okay?\n"
        }
      ],
      "reacted_message_ts": "1781173097.897099",
      "reaction_type": "obsidian-jg"
    },
    {
      "channel_id": "D0968B1BR98",
      "channel_name": "dm-Kristina",
      "ts": "1781174712.027409",
      "permalink": "https://sign-lab.slack.com/archives/D0968B1BR98/p1781174712027409",
      "is_thread": false,
      "messages": [
        {
          "ts": "1781092132.138639",
          "user_id": "U06RDKU1N3E",
          "user_name": "Kristina",
          "text": "I can join for our call if you want "
        },
        {
          "ts": "1781092134.603799",
          "user_id": "U06RDKU1N3E",
          "user_name": "Kristina",
          "text": "I'm in the airport "
        },
        {
          "ts": "1781092139.501649",
          "user_id": "U06RDKU1N3E",
          "user_name": "Kristina",
          "text": "I leave in 3 hours "
        },
        {
          "ts": "1781092140.918739",
          "user_id": "U06RDKU1N3E",
          "user_name": "Kristina",
          "text": "No topics "
        },
        {
          "ts": "1781092475.246399",
          "user_id": "U095LHMC4UW",
          "user_name": "Justin",
          "text": "Have a great trip!!!"
        },
        {
          "ts": "1781174712.027409",
          "user_id": "U095LHMC4UW",
          "user_name": "Justin",
          "text": "(not urgent) for the weekly meeting, let's do a joint bug-report-and-triage discussion? I'll ask you to talk a bit about any notable observations from support tickets, then we'll look at Linear issues in triage. (I've wanted to formalize this process for a while now, seems like a good opportunity.)\n\nWhat do you think? Will you help me remember to do this for the next few weeks? :joy:"
        }
      ],
      "reacted_message_ts": "1781174712.027409",
      "reaction_type": "obsidian-jg"
    },
    {
      "channel_id": "C09925943PY",
      "channel_name": "mpdm-justin--tor--anya-1",
      "ts": "1781130149.372509",
      "permalink": "https://sign-lab.slack.com/archives/C09925943PY/p1781130149372509",
      "is_thread": false,
      "messages": [
        {
          "ts": "1780499459.549859",
          "user_id": "U095LHMC4UW",
          "user_name": "Justin",
          "text": "I'm actually out tomorrow for last-day-of-school stuff, but I think you two should meet without me. Just send me a recording and some notes and I'll catch up on Friday!"
        },
        {
          "ts": "1780665245.993859",
          "user_id": "U095LHMC4UW",
          "user_name": "Justin",
          "text": "Did you two get a chance to chat yet? Any notes or recordings I can review?"
        },
        {
          "ts": "1780891851.262489",
          "user_id": "U7DJZAQ3G",
          "user_name": "Tor",
          "text": "We forgot to record I think.\n\n\u2022 I brought up as a topic that maybe we would want \"3D\" task-buttons, and that this is something to perhaps think about sooner rather later, since it would interfere with the animations. Anya mentioned some reasons why we perhaps would _not_ want those buttons to be 3D, but also wanted to not be hasty to jump towards a particular conclusion.\n\u2022 We had a small screenshare session where Anya prompted Claude.\n    \u25e6 It turned out that the existing animations Anya had worked on were \"web only\" (while it's preferably to aim towards something more universal). Anya will try further iterations where things are not predictably \"web only\".\n\u2022 I asked about what she's working on (Storybook and so on), and ended up slightly/moderately more confused compared to how it was before I asked. I expressed skepticism against making a big/significant bet on trying to create a competing source of truth to the code-base itself in regards to design, while at the same time making clear that me not even knowing what \"the vision\" is speaks in favor of being more agnostic than I otherwise might have been.\n    \u25e6 I also mentioned that probably I'm less bullish than other people on the team in regards to outside help from \"experts\" (the context being that an outsider is involved somehow in this new competing source of truth that is envisioned)."
        },
        {
          "ts": "1780899268.488099",
          "user_id": "U02SSCPM1MH",
          "user_name": "Anya Volosskaya",
          "text": "Thank you so much, Tor!\n\nHere is a summary from Granola re product:\n\n\u2022 Anya shared progress on the *jumping answer chips* animation experiment. The current prototype works on *web only* and has been pushed to a remote branch for review.The experiment was built with *Claude-assisted coding* and documented in Linear. The main open question is whether this workflow is effective for producing work that is useful beyond early prototyping.\n\u2022 Main technical issue: the current implementation uses *CSS/web-specific animation*, so it does *not yet transfer to iOS/Android*. The team discussed moving toward animation solutions that are more likely to work across platforms in React Native.\n\u2022 The core tradeoff discussed was:\n    \u25e6 *visual prototyping value*: fast to explore and align on motion in a realistic UI\n    \u25e6 *implementation value*: higher value if the work is portable to the final product stackTwo animation directions are currently preferred:\n\u201c*springy plus last style*\u201d\n\u201c*sparkle wave plus tense*\u201d\n\n\u2022 Next step is to continue experimentation with a stronger *cross-platform constraint from the start*, rather than treating web exploration as the main path.\n\u2022 Tor\u2019s view was that animation exploration is useful, but the highest-value work is finding something that has a *good chance of working on iOS and Android*, not just looking good on web.\n\u2022 There was also a related design discussion about whether answer chips should become *3D* like some other interactive elements. No decision was made. Anya\u2019s current lean is to *keep them flat for now* because they serve a different interaction purpose and that may help preserve visual hierarchy.\nImmediate plan:\nAnya may continue experimenting - focus on getting the iOS to work\nTor has access to the current branch\nTor and Anya plan to reconnect early next week and reassess progress"
        },
        {
          "ts": "1780899339.532529",
          "user_id": "U02SSCPM1MH",
          "user_name": "Anya Volosskaya",
          "text": "CC @Justin :raised_hands:"
        },
        {
          "ts": "1781130149.372509",
          "user_id": "U02SSCPM1MH",
          "user_name": "Anya Volosskaya",
          "text": "Hi, team!\n\nUpdate on the correct answer chips animation in lessons :black_nib:  I have added <https://linear.app/signlab/issue/PROD-468/animate-correct-answers-in-lessons#comment-f4c8b593|all details on Linear> starting from the linked comment and further down.\n\nI have done another round of work and exploration and via using Reanimated was able to get the behaviour we would like on the simulator.\nFrom my understanding Reanimated module should be a good fit for us, Tor, that is one of my core questions :nerd_face:\n\nSome more details about Reanimated:"
        },
        {
          "ts": "1781130519.747669",
          "user_id": "U02SSCPM1MH",
          "user_name": "Anya Volosskaya",
          "text": "@Justin, here is a short summary.\n@Tor, feel free to dive straight into Linear details\n\n*We got:* Two polished animations for the sign-sequence task \u2014 the answer tiles spring/wave and the last one lands with a punchy flourish. Built in React Native using Reanimated (the standard, production-grade RN animation library).\n\n*What works according to Claude and I* :crossed_fingers: \n\u2022 iOS \u2014 natively. Verified on the iOS Simulator: the real animation is smooth.\n\u2022 Web \u2014 via `react-native-web` (Expo web): the _same_ React Native + Reanimated code, rendered in the browser, inside a real lesson with real sign videos.\n\u2022 One cross-platform codebase.The same component targets iOS, Android, and web \u2014 no platform-specific code.\n\u2022 Android:not yet run on an Android device, but it\u2019s the _identical_ code path, so it\u2019s expected to work; this needs a quick low-end-Android perf check.\n*To ship to the live apps (from my understanding):* a one-time setup \u2014 add Reanimated to the native builds and rebuild once (can ride the next release). After that, this animation and all future animations ship as normal JS.\n\n*Risk according to Claude* :robot_face: low. Reanimated is a standard RN library, already half-declared in our deps; the change is additive (should not break existing screens). Only watch-item: that low-end-Android perf pass.\n\n*Where it lives:* branch `D0_experiment_reanimated_jumping_tiles` \u2192 start with `client/app/reanimatedNativeTest/ENGINEERING_HANDOFF.md`."
        }
      ],
      "reacted_message_ts": "1781130149.372509",
      "reaction_type": "obsidian-jg"
    },

  "linear": [
    {
      "type": "Comment",
      "id": "27ad0368-ab2d-42e7-aae1-e191f22b1e24",
      "body": "(Left this in Figma too): I prefer wavyEven but I'd recommend making it all a bit faster. I'd suggest that we try to keep the animation at under 1s for moderate answer lengths. Just kind of feels right?",
      "createdAt": "2026-06-11T10:43:41.278Z",
      "url": "https://linear.app/signlab/issue/PROD-468/animate-correct-answers-in-lessons#comment-27ad0368",
      "author": "Justin",
      "reactions": [
        {
          "emoji": "obsidian_jg",
          "user": {
            "id": "211987db-f790-4bfa-8c8e-518e1f704901",
            "name": "Justin"
          }
        }
      ],
      "context": {
        "issue": {
          "id": "4df8dcfe-c236-49fc-8852-f552cfe67a85",
          "identifier": "PROD-468",
          "title": "Animate correct answers in lessons",
          "description": "### Summary\n\nWe want to try some subtle Duolingo-style animations for correct answers to see if it improves Day 0 \u2192 Day 1 retention. We think this might help *and* we think this might help us understand how much impact this kind of improvement can have on retention.\n\n### Hypothesis\n\nAdding animations to celebrate success makes the app more fun, which in turn makes new users more likely to engage on Day 0 and come back on Day 1.\n\n### Variants\n\n* Control: No animations for correct answers.\n* Experiment: Subtle animations for correct answers.\n  * Correct answers gently bounce as the correct answer drawer slides in.\n  * For incorrect answers, the drawer still slides in, but there's no bounce.\n\n### Test Plan\n\n* Target: increase in d0 content exhaustion *or* increase in d1 retention\n* Guardrail(s): Lesson completion rates\n* Experiment **wins** if:\n  * d1 retention *and* d0 content exhaustion are neutral or positive\n  * Lesson completion rates are neutral or positive\n* Rationale: We think that subtle moments of fun like this will add up, even if by themselves they don't cause statistically significant improvements.\n\n### Results\n\n* \n* \n\n### Decision\n\n* ",
          "url": "https://linear.app/signlab/issue/PROD-468/animate-correct-answers-in-lessons",
          "state": {
            "name": "In Progress"
          },
          "project": {
            "id": "0a533d83-6a7d-47e6-9929-b175a50eb6a9",
            "name": "Day 0 \u2192 Day 1 Quick Tests",
            "description": "A set of low-cost, low-regret tweaks and tests we try ASAP"
          },
          "comments": {
            "nodes": [
              {
                "id": "27ad0368-ab2d-42e7-aae1-e191f22b1e24",
                "body": "(Left this in Figma too): I prefer wavyEven but I'd recommend making it all a bit faster. I'd suggest that we try to keep the animation at under 1s for moderate answer lengths. Just kind of feels right?",
                "createdAt": "2026-06-11T10:43:41.278Z",
                "user": {
                  "name": "Justin"
                }
              },
              {
                "id": "f6c9abec-c66f-471e-8c3a-afd04b92304b",
                "body": "Note: at the moment this has been created for `SEE_SIGN_SENTENCE_SPECIFY_SIGN_SEQUENCE` task, if this is the right direction, I can look at the other task types. And/or look at the drawer.",
                "createdAt": "2026-06-10T22:10:23.717Z",
                "user": {
                  "name": "Anya"
                }
              },
              {
                "id": "3d94c36e-acae-47c8-bc13-0b6789546c2d",
                "body": "The visualizations of what I got: \n\nWe have two versions, I think either works very well \ud83d\ude4c I do have a medium preference toward **wavyEven** \u2014 \"No-sparkle wave + tense end\", especially for the longer sentences.\n\n* **wavyEven** \u2014 \"No-sparkle wave + tense end\": a sparkle-free travelling wave across the tiles, with a held, punchy finale (sparkles) on the last tile.\n* **mixedB** \u2014 \"Springy + last tile extra punchy\": a springy hop on every tile (all spark), with the same punchy finale on the last tile. And when the sparkle is only at the end, we get an extra \u2018umf\u2019.\n  \n  @tor @justin \n\n  [Simulator_1.mov](https://uploads.linear.app/bfeb8dae-7c0e-4f46-abbb-0ebfa5e11075/65460190-768f-4f32-9f4f-4ac903694b57/fc8783f5-d040-4c98-b5ae-b8d917810d56)\n\n  [ReactWeb_with our design 1.mov](https://uploads.linear.app/bfeb8dae-7c0e-4f46-abbb-0ebfa5e11075/523b89bb-81f6-4e61-8bc3-0f8239d5edfd/544ad774-064b-4dce-bafb-dd2dcc0ded4e)\n\n  \n  And you can [see the full the journey here](<https://www.figma.com/board/QVaKaxNPZygFDstVmRdaKq/Answer-chips-animated---process?node-id=3-31&t=fOxEXvVPG7BGApY9-1>) - very draft form, but you can see the progress \ud83d\udc40",
                "createdAt": "2026-06-10T22:08:00.612Z",
                "user": {
                  "name": "Anya"
                }
              },
              {
                "id": "b9e94a5e-8529-45a2-8a94-a8beb0e2ddec",
                "body": "I am not sure if adding the reanimated module is a bog deal or not, here is what Claude says: \n\nNote, that I tried using RN Animated, and it was much less exciting, so if possible, Reanimated is very much an unlock for further animations :partywizard: @tor @justin\n\n![image.png](https://uploads.linear.app/bfeb8dae-7c0e-4f46-abbb-0ebfa5e11075/7cef2ef7-b357-4d89-aaf2-ca45cd1aa348/65164430-1af2-4e2d-b37c-b3de6a2d826c)",
                "createdAt": "2026-06-10T21:48:55.280Z",
                "user": {
                  "name": "Anya"
                }
              },
              {
                "id": "f4c8b593-b410-413e-91b2-8912a991883d",
                "body": "Hi @tor!\n\nHere is the next milestone from me, I hope it will get us a step closer \ud83e\udd1e\n\n> **TL;DR:** React Native + Reanimated, verified on iOS sim and web. To ship: add `react-native-reanimated` + `react-native-worklets` to the native builds, rebuild once, wire into the answer area. Details in the handoff doc.\n\n---\n\n**Branch:** `D0_experiment_reanimated_jumping_tiles` (pushed to origin)\n\n**Get it:**\n\n```\ngit fetch origin\ngit switch D0_experiment_reanimated_jumping_tiles\n```\n\n**Read first \u2014 full spec (install/build/integrate):**\n`client/app/reanimatedNativeTest/ENGINEERING_HANDOFF.md`\n(view on GitHub: [https://github.com/EndreElv/SignLab-Dev/blob/D0_experiment_reanimated_jumping_tiles/client/app/reanimatedNativeTest/ENGINEERING_HANDOFF.md](<https://github.com/EndreElv/SignLab-Dev/blob/D0_experiment_reanimated_jumping_tiles/client/app/reanimatedNativeTest/ENGINEERING_HANDOFF.md>))\n\n**Key files:**\n\n* Tuned animation (source of truth): `client/app/components/learnContent/taskTypes/textButtonOptions/JumpingTilesMotionReanimated.tsx`\n* Real-chip wrapper + wiring: `AnimatedTaskOptionGlossChip.tsx` + `SignSequenceAnswerArea.tsx` (same folder)\n\n**See it run:**\n\n* Web (real task + real video): `cd client && yarn web` \u2192 open `http://localhost:19006/jumping-tiles`\n* iOS (native, standalone preview): `cd client && expo run:ios`\n\n---\n\nCC @justin for visibility \ud83d\ude4c\n\n[ENGINEERING_HANDOFF.md](https://uploads.linear.app/bfeb8dae-7c0e-4f46-abbb-0ebfa5e11075/9b168bbd-4556-4f6a-acc1-cb2d98febc2c/a1f184da-1b98-4eed-8490-6da5b2f85b44)",
                "createdAt": "2026-06-10T21:48:15.987Z",
                "user": {
                  "name": "Anya"
                }
              },
              {
                "id": "323f92a2-8002-4957-9311-751d486c026d",
                "body": "I have not worked on the sliding drawer, I will chat with Tor and then will work on that.",
                "createdAt": "2026-06-04T14:09:58.767Z",
                "user": {
                  "name": "Anya"
                }
              },
              {
                "id": "8150caa2-6e94-44ef-bd0d-bb592556eae6",
                "body": "Below will add the options that we have to visualize what we got \u2712\ufe0f  \n@justin @tor\n\n[Springy + last tile extra punchy..mov](https://uploads.linear.app/bfeb8dae-7c0e-4f46-abbb-0ebfa5e11075/91bf5fc5-0e82-48c8-b19d-a8af82fa4ee9/2811660e-5ba7-4f6c-a48e-d999a9474cfb)\n\n[No sparkle wave + tense end.mov](https://uploads.linear.app/bfeb8dae-7c0e-4f46-abbb-0ebfa5e11075/40561e0b-fd8e-4b0a-929e-ed9d71198d70/3c25c28f-7730-4286-8586-051eef109f00)",
                "createdAt": "2026-06-04T14:09:19.668Z",
                "user": {
                  "name": "Anya"
                }
              },
              {
                "id": "75cf0bf1-31e4-4f61-b3e8-cff832789875",
                "body": "Hi @tor, in preparation fro our chat today, the experiment is available on the remote branch \ud83d\udcaa \nThese are some hand-off tips for you from Claude:\n\n**Jumping-tiles correct-answer animation experiment**\n\nBranch: `D0_experiment_jumping_tiles` (pushed to `origin`, not merged anywhere \u2014 does not touch `master`).\n\nGet it running:\n\n```\ngit fetch origin\ngit checkout D0_experiment_jumping_tiles\ncd client && yarn web\n```\n\nThen open the `jumping-tiles` route (e.g. `http://localhost:19006/jumping-tiles`). Use the floating control overlay (centre-left) to switch variants, **New** to reroll, then submit a correct answer to see the animation.\n\nFull context in `JumpingTiles_handoff/README.md` \u2014 architecture, the variant list, and the main caveat:\n\n\u26a0\ufe0f **The animations are web-only right now.** They're CSS `@keyframes` in `AnimatedTaskOptionGlossChip.web.tsx`; the native `.tsx` is a static fallback (no animation on iOS/Android). Making them smooth on all three platforms means reimplementing the motion with `react-native-reanimated` (native-thread transforms/opacity). The README has the details.\n\n---\n\n@justin @tor I have focused on the tension effect in the new iterations \u263a\ufe0f \n\nMy favourites are:\n\n1. Springy + last tile extra punchy \n2. No sparkle wave + tense end",
                "createdAt": "2026-06-04T14:05:09.865Z",
                "user": {
                  "name": "Anya"
                }
              },
              {
                "id": "07b601a7-ecd0-4d77-bb8f-29d0d5bd30f0",
                "body": "@anya Let's put this monkey on your back while @tor works on some other, higher priorities that don't require the same degree of design input.",
                "createdAt": "2026-05-28T16:21:21.033Z",
                "user": {
                  "name": "Justin"
                }
              }
            ]
          }

Details in the handoff doc.\n\n---\n\n**Branch:** `D0_experiment_reanimated_jumping_tiles` (pushed to origin)\n\n**Get it:**\n\n```\ngit fetch origin\ngit switch D0_experiment_reanimated_jumping_tiles\n```\n\n**Read first \u2014 full spec (install/build/integrate):**\n`client/app/reanimatedNativeTest/ENGINEERING_HANDOFF.md`\n(view on GitHub: [https://github.com/EndreElv/SignLab-Dev/blob/D0_experiment_reanimated_jumping_tiles/client/app/reanimatedNativeTest/ENGINEERING_HANDOFF.md](<https://github.com/EndreElv/SignLab-Dev/blob/D0_experiment_reanimated_jumping_tiles/client/app/reanimatedNativeTest/ENGINEERING_HANDOFF.md>))\n\n**Key files:**\n\n* Tuned animation (source of truth): `client/app/components/learnContent/taskTypes/textButtonOptions/JumpingTilesMotionReanimated.tsx`\n* Real-chip wrapper + wiring: `AnimatedTaskOptionGlossChip.tsx` + `SignSequenceAnswerArea.tsx` (same folder)\n\n**See it run:**\n\n* Web (real task + real video): `cd client && yarn web` \u2192 open `http://localhost:19006/jumping-tiles`\n* iOS (native, standalone preview): `cd client && expo run:ios`\n\n---\n\nCC @justin for visibility \ud83d\ude4c\n\n[ENGINEERING_HANDOFF.md](https://uploads.linear.app/bfeb8dae-7c0e-4f46-abbb-0ebfa5e11075/9b168bbd-4556-4f6a-acc1-cb2d98febc2c/a1f184da-1b98-4eed-8490-6da5b2f85b44)",
      "createdAt": "2026-06-10T21:48:15.987Z",
      "url": "https://linear.app/signlab/issue/PROD-468/animate-correct-answers-in-lessons#comment-f4c8b593",
      "author": "Anya",
      "reactions": [
        {
          "emoji": "obsidian_jg",

            "id": "211987db-f790-4bfa-8c8e-518e1f704901",

      "context": {
        "issue": {
          "id": "4df8dcfe-c236-49fc-8852-f552cfe67a85",
          "identifier": "PROD-468",
          "title": "Animate correct answers in lessons",
          "description": "### Summary\n\nWe want to try some subtle Duolingo-style animations for correct answers to see if it improves Day 0 \u2192 Day 1 retention. We think this might help *and* we think this might help us understand how much impact this kind of improvement can have on retention.\n\n### Hypothesis\n\nAdding animations to celebrate success makes the app more fun, which in turn makes new users more likely to engage on Day 0 and come back on Day 1.\n\n### Variants\n\n* Control: No animations for correct answers.\n* Experiment: Subtle animations for correct answers.\n  * Correct answers gently bounce as the correct answer drawer slides in.\n  * For incorrect answers, the drawer still slides in, but there's no bounce.\n\n### Test Plan\n\n* Target: increase in d0 content exhaustion *or* increase in d1 retention\n* Guardrail(s): Lesson completion rates\n* Experiment **wins** if:\n  * d1 retention *and* d0 content exhaustion are neutral or positive\n  * Lesson completion rates are neutral or positive\n* Rationale: We think that subtle moments of fun like this will add up, even if by themselves they don't cause statistically significant improvements.\n\n### Results\n\n* \n* \n\n### Decision\n\n* ",
          "url": "https://linear.app/signlab/issue/PROD-468/animate-correct-answers-in-lessons",
          "state": {
            "name": "In Progress"
          },
          "project": {
            "id": "0a533d83-6a7d-47e6-9929-b175a50eb6a9",
            "name": "Day 0 \u2192 Day 1 Quick Tests",
            "description": "A set of low-cost, low-regret tweaks and tests we try ASAP"
          },
          "comments": {
            "nodes": [
              {
                "id": "27ad0368-ab2d-42e7-aae1-e191f22b1e24",
                "body": "(Left this in Figma too): I prefer wavyEven but I'd recommend making it all a bit faster. I'd suggest that we try to keep the animation at under 1s for moderate answer lengths. Just kind of feels right?",
                "createdAt": "2026-06-11T10:43:41.278Z",
                "user": {
                  "name": "Justin"
                }
              },
              {
                "id": "f6c9abec-c66f-471e-8c3a-afd04b92304b",

And when the sparkle is only at the end, we get an extra \u2018umf\u2019.\n  \n  @tor @justin \n\n  [Simulator_1.mov](https://uploads.linear.app/bfeb8dae-7c0e-4f46-abbb-0ebfa5e11075/65460190-768f-4f32-9f4f-4ac903694b57/fc8783f5-d040-4c98-b5ae-b8d917810d56)\n\n  [ReactWeb_with our design 1.mov](https://uploads.linear.app/bfeb8dae-7c0e-4f46-abbb-0ebfa5e11075/523b89bb-81f6-4e61-8bc3-0f8239d5edfd/544ad774-064b-4dce-bafb-dd2dcc0ded4e)\n\n  \n  And you can [see the full the journey here](<https://www.figma.com/board/QVaKaxNPZygFDstVmRdaKq/Answer-chips-animated---process?node-id=3-31&t=fOxEXvVPG7BGApY9-1>) - very draft form, but you can see the progress \ud83d\udc40",

    {
      "session_id": "20260605_161844_72d57c63",
      "title": "Automating Slack to Vault Notes",
      "started_at": "2026-06-05T16:18:44.258141",
      "source_db": "/home/justin.guest/.hermes/state.db",
      "message_count": 139,
      "messages": [
        {
          "role": "user",
          "content": "I will sometimes have Slack conversations that should probably be turned into notes in my vault, something like sources/slack. I don't have a way to forward these to you like I would for emails. Is there a way we could automate this without adding too much noise to my vault? I'm thinking that these would all be the same kind of Slack conversations that are worth mentioning in the work log. Maybe we just capture them there? What would the pros and cons be of spinning out separate notes for those conversations ...",
          "timestamp": 1780690841.3594227,
          "message_id": null
        },
        {
          "role": "assistant",
          "content": "",
          "timestamp": 1780690841.3615592,
          "message_id": null
        },
        {
          "role": "tool",
          "content": "{\"success\": true, \"name\": \"slack\", \"description\": \"Operate Justin's Slack on his behalf via user-token (xoxp-) API \u2014 read channels/DMs/threads, search messages, post as Justin, react. Workspace: SignLab. Single-workspace today; designed to multi-tenant later.\", \"tags\": [\"slack\", \"signlab\", \"messaging\", \"second-brain\"], \"related_skills\": [\"obsidian\", \"google-workspace\"], \"content\": \"---\\nname: slack\\ndescription: \\\"Operate Justin's Slack on his behalf via user-token (xoxp-) API \u2014 read channels/DMs/threads, search messages, post as Justin, react. Workspace: SignLab. Single-workspace today; designed to multi-tenant later.\\\"\\nversion: 1.0.0\\nauthor: Bes\\nlicense: MIT\\nmetadata:\\n  hermes:\\n    tags: [slack, signlab, messaging, second-brain]\\n    related_skills: [obsidian, google-workspace]\\n---\\n\\n# Slack (SignLab workspace, user-token mode)\\n\\n## \u26a0\ufe0f READ FIRST \u2014 operating posture\\n\\nThis Slack access is **user-token** (`xoxp-`), not a bot. That means:\\n\\n- **Anything you post appears as Justin himself**, not as \\\"Bes\\\". There is no\\n  visible distinction in Slack between \\\"Justin typed this on his phone\\\" and\\n  \\\"Bes posted this via the API on Justin's behalf.\\\" Co-workers will react\\n  to your posts as if Justin wrote them.\\n- You can read every channel, DM, and group DM Justin can read. That\\n  includes potentially sensitive conversations. Treat what you see as you\\n  would treat reading Justin's email: extract what's useful, don't quote\\n  verbatim to third parties.\\n- **Default to read-only operations.** Posting and reacting are allowed\\n  but require either (a) Justin explicitly asking (\\\"post X in #foo\\\") or\\n  (b) a cron/skill flow that Justin has previously approved.\\n- **Never post in a public channel without confirming with Justin first**\\n  unless the post is in a channel he's explicitly designated for\\n  automation (none today).\\n\\n## Workspace\\n\\n- **SignLab** (only workspace today)\\n- Auth: env var `SLACK_USER_TOKEN` (starts with `xoxp-`)\\n- App name in Slack: \\\"Bes\\\" (or similar) \u2014 Justin owns it\\n- Scopes installed: channels/groups/im/mpim history+read, users:read,\\n  search:read, files:read, reactions:read, chat:write, reactions:write\\n\\nIf a write op 403s with `missing_scope`, the response includes the needed\\nscope. Tell Justin which scope is missing rather than guessing.\\n\\n## The wrapper: `slack` CLI\\n\\nThere is a thin Python wrapper at `scripts/slack.py` (installed onto PATH\\nas `slack`). Call it via the standard pattern:\\n\\n```bash\\n~/.hermes/hermes-agent/venv/bin/python \\\\\\n  ~/.hermes/skills/social-media/slack/scripts/slack.py <subcommand> [args]\\n```\\n\\nAll subcommands print JSON to stdout by default (parseable). Use `--text`\\nfor human-readable rendering of message lists.\\n\\n### Subcommands\\n\\n#### `whoami`\\nReturns the authed user's identity. Sanity-check the token works:\\n```bash\\nslack whoami\\n# {\\\"user_id\\\": \\\"U01ABC...\\\", \\\"team_id\\\": \\\"T01...\\\", \\\"team\\\": \\\"SignLab\\\", \\\"user\\\": \\\"justin\\\"}\\n```\\n\\n#### `channels [--type public|private|im|mpim|all] [--member-only] [--limit N]`\\nList channels. Default: `--type all --member-only` (only conversations\\nJustin is in \u2014 usually what you want).\\n```bash\\nslack channels --type public --member-only\\n# [{\\\"id\\\": \\\"C01...\\\", \\\"name\\\": \\\"general\\\", \\\"is_member\\\": true, \\\"is_private\\\": false, \\\"num_members\\\": 42}, ...]\\n```\\n\\nUse `--type im` to list DMs, `--type mpim` for group DMs. IM entries\\ninclude `user` (the other person's user ID).\\n\\n#### `read <channel_id> [--since 24h|2d|2025-05-15] [--limit N] [--text]`\\nFetch recent messages from a channel/DM/thread. Resolves user IDs to\\ndisplay names automatically.\\n```bash\\nslack read C0123456789 --since 24h --text\\n# 2026-05-20 09:14  alice: heads up, the deploy is rolling\\n# 2026-05-20 09:16  bob:   ack\\n```\\n\\n`--since` accepts: `Nh` / `Nd` / `Nw` (relative) or `YYYY-MM-DD` (absolute,\\nmidnight UTC). Default: `24h`.\\n\\nJSON mode (no `--text`) returns each message with `ts`, `user`, `user_name`,\\n`text`, `thread_ts`, `reply_count`, `reactions`.\\n\\n#### `thread <channel_id> <thread_ts> [--text]`\\nFetch a full thread (parent + all replies).\\n```bash\\nslack thread C0123 1716200000.123456 --text\\n```\\n\\n#### `search <query> [--limit N] [--text]`\\nUse Slack's `search.messages` endpoint. Supports Slack search modifiers:\\n`from:@alice`, `in:#general`, `before:2025-05-01`, `after:`, `has::eyes:`,\\n`-from:@bot`, quoted phrases, etc.\\n```bash\\nslack search 'in:#engineering deploy after:2025-05-15' --limit 20\\n```\\n\\nJSON mode returns hits with `permalink` (so you can cite back to Justin in\\nTelegram).\\n\\n#### `post <channel_id> <text> [--thread <ts>] [--broadcast]`\\nPost a message. **Posts as Justin.** Confirm with Justin first unless the\\nask is explicit.\\n```bash\\nslack post C0123 \\\"Will follow up after the 2pm.\\\"\\nslack post C0123 \\\"ack\\\" --thread 1716200000.123456\\n```\\n\\n`--broadcast` (only with `--thread`): also surface the thread reply to the\\nmain channel (\\\"Also send to channel\\\" in the Slack UI).\\n\\nReturns `{\\\"ok\\\": true, \\\"ts\\\": \\\"1716200099.123\\\", \\\"channel\\\": \\\"C0123\\\",\\n\\\"permalink\\\": \\\"https://signlab.slack.com/archives/C0123/p17162...\\\"}`.\\n\\n#### `react <channel_id> <message_ts> <emoji>`\\nAdd a reaction. `emoji` without colons (`thumbsup`, not `:thumbsup:`).\\n```bash\\nslack react C0123 1716200000.123456 eyes\\n```\\n\\n#### `permalink <channel_id> <message_ts>`\\nGet a permalink for citation. Often the most useful op when answering\\nJustin: cite the source.\\n\\n#### `user <user_id>`\\nResolve a user ID to profile info (name, real_name, email if available,\\ntitle, status). Cached for the duration of the process.\\n\\n#### `dm <user_id_or_name> <text>`\\nConvenience: opens (or finds) a DM with the user and posts. `user_id_or_name`\\naccepts either `U01ABC...` or `@alice` (resolved via users.list).\\n\\n### Common recipes\\n\\n**\\\"What did I miss in #foo today?\\\"**\\n1. `slack channels --type public --member-only` to find the channel ID.\\n2. `slack read <id> --since 24h --text`.\\n3. Summarize for Justin in Telegram. If you cite specific messages, include\\n   the permalink so he can jump.\\n\\n**\\\"Did anyone @ me?\\\"**\\n1. `slack search '@<justin_user_id>' --limit 30`. Or, more reliably:\\n2. Iterate Justin's member-channels, fetch recent messages, filter for\\n   `<@U_justin>` in text. The search endpoint sometimes misses mentions in\\n   private channels depending on indexing.\\n\\n**\\\"What do I have reminders set on?\\\"**\\nUse `has:reminder` in a search query \u2014 this is the supported workaround\\nfor the missing `reminders:read` scope (see Pitfalls). It returns messages\\nJustin has flagged with Slack's native reminder feature:\\n```bash\\nslack search 'has:reminder after:2026-05-19' --limit 50\\n```\\nNote: the literal word \\\"reminder\\\" appearing in message *text* can\\noccasionally bleed in (e.g. \\\"Reminder: meeting at 3pm\\\"). Use channel\\ncontext to distinguish these from genuinely reminder-flagged messages.\\n\\n**\\\"Reply to Alice's DM\\\"**\\n1. `slack channels --type im` to find the DM channel ID for Alice's user_id.\\n2. `slack read <dm_id> --since 24h --text` to read context.\\n3. Confirm content with Justin in Telegram (paraphrase his ask).\\n4. `slack post <dm_id> \\\"<message>\\\"`.\\n\\n**\\\"Find the discussion about $TOPIC\\\"**\\n1. `slack search '$TOPIC' --limit 20`.\\n2. Pick the relevant thread, grab `channel` and `thread_ts` (use `ts` if no\\n   thread_ts).\\n3. `slack thread <channel> <thread_ts> --text` for full context.\\n\\n## Pitfalls\\n\\n1. **Permalinks for users not in the channel resolve to a 404 in Slack.**\\n   If you cite a permalink from #private-channel to a Slack viewer not in\\n   that channel, they'll see \\\"Channel not found.\\\" For Justin himself this\\n   is fine \u2014 he can see anything he can read. Watch out if you ever\\n   summarize for someone else.\\n\\n2. **`search.messages` excludes some recently-edited or recently-posted\\n   messages** due to Slack's search index lag (~minutes). For \\\"right now\\\"\\n   queries on a specific channel, use `read --since 1h` instead of search.\\n\\n3. **Thread `ts` vs message `ts`**. A reply has its own `ts` AND a\\n   `thread_ts` pointing to the parent. To reply IN a thread, you pass the\\n   parent's `ts` (which IS the thread_ts). To react TO a specific reply,\\n   you pass the reply's own `ts`. Read both fields from JSON output before\\n   choosing which one to pass downstream.\\n\\n4. **Direct messages are channels too** \u2014 their IDs start with `D`. The\\n   `channels:history` scope does NOT cover DMs; `im:history` does. If a\\n   `read` against a `D...` ID returns `missing_scope`, the token wasn't\\n   installed with `im:history`.\\n\\n5. **Posting in a channel you're not a member of fails by default** even\\n   with `chat:write`. You'd need `chat:write.public` (deliberately not\\n   granted). For now: `slack channels --member-only` to see where posting\\n   will work.\\n\\n6. **Display name vs username vs real name.** `user.name` is the legacy\\n   username (often the email prefix); `user.profile.display_name` is what\\n   appears in the UI; `user.profile.real_name` is the full name. The\\n   wrapper prefers `display_name` > `real_name` > `name` when rendering.\\n\\n7. **Rate limits**. User tokens are on Tier 2-3 rate limits (~20-50\\n   requests/minute depending on method). For \\\"scan everything Justin is\\n   in\\\", batch and add a small `time.sleep(1)` between channel reads.\\n\\n8. **`reminders:read` scope is not installed.** Calling `reminders.list`\\n   via the Slack SDK will fail with `missing_scope`. The installed token\\n   does not have this scope. Use `has:reminder` in a search query as a\\n   workaround (see \\\"What do I have reminders set on?\\\" in Common recipes).\\n   To get the real scope, Justin would need to re-authorize the Slack app\\n   with `reminders:read` added.\\n\\n## Adding a second workspace later\\n\\nWhen/if Justin adds Nous Research or another workspace, the design path is:\\n1. Create a second Slack app in that workspace, get a second `xoxp-` token.\\n2. Add `SLACK_USER_TOKEN_NOUS=*** to `.env` (one env var per\\n   workspace, distinguished by suffix).\\n3. Patch `scripts/slack.py` to accept `--workspace signlab|nous` and route\\n   to the corresponding env var. Default workspace = SignLab.\\n4. Update this SKILL.md with the workspace list.\\n\\nPattern mirrors `gws_multi.py` in the google-workspace skill.\\n\\n## Where things live\\n\\n- **Token**: `~/.hermes/.env` \u2192 `SLACK_USER_TOKEN`\\n- **Wrapper script**: `~/.hermes/skills/social-media/slack/scripts/slack.py`\\n- **Convenience symlink** (optional): `~/.local/bin/slack` \u2192 wrapper\\n- **Python deps**: `slack_sdk` installed in `~/.hermes/hermes-agent/venv`\\n- **Workspace home**: signlab.slack.com\\n\", \"path\": \"social-media/slack/SKILL.md\", \"skill_dir\": \"/home/justin.guest/.hermes/skills/social-media/slack\", \"linked_files\": {\"scripts\": [\"scripts/slack.py\"]}, \"usage_hint\": \"To view linked files, call skill_view(name, file_path) where file_path is e.g. 'references/api.md' or 'assets/config.yaml'\", \"required_environment_variables\": [], \"required_commands\": [], \"missing_required_environment_variables\": [], \"missing_credential_files\": [], \"missing_required_commands\": [], \"setup_needed\": false, \"setup_skipped\": false, \"readiness_status\": \"available\", \"metadata\": {\"hermes\": {\"tags\": [\"slack\", \"signlab\", \"messaging\", \"second-brain\"], \"related_skills\": [\"obsidian\", \"google-workspace\"]}}}",
          "timestamp": 1780690841.3639255,
          "message_id": null
        },

          "role": "assistant",

        {
          "role": "tool",
          "content": "{\"success\": true, \"name\": \"work-log\", \"description\": \"Use when Justin asks to \\\"create a work log\\\", \\\"log today's work\\\", \\\"write a work log\\\", or otherwise wants today's work activity summarized and appended to today's daily note in the Obsidian vault. Pulls from Slack (SignLab), Linear, Gmail (work + personal-main; personal-junk only on request), Google Calendar (all 3 accounts), Todoist (completed + due-today tasks), plus the daily note and any chat brain-dump.\", \"tags\": [], \"related_skills\": [], \"content\": \"---\\nname: work-log\\ndescription: Use when Justin asks to \\\"create a work log\\\", \\\"log today's work\\\", \\\"write a work log\\\", or otherwise wants today's work activity summarized and appended to today's daily note in the Obsidian vault. Pulls from Slack (SignLab), Linear, Gmail (work + personal-main; personal-junk only on request), Google Calendar (all 3 accounts), Todoist (completed + due-today tasks), plus the daily note and any chat brain-dump.\\nplatforms: [linux, macos]\\n---\\n\\n# \ud83d\udccb Work Log\\n\\nSummarize today's work activity and append a structured Work Log block to **today's daily note** in the Obsidian vault. Do NOT create a separate file.\\n\\nThe block has three sections \u2014 **Highlights / Decisions / Open Questions** \u2014 synthesized across all sources. New sources do not get their own headings; they feed the synthesis. The footer enumerates which sources were actually pulled and rough counts.\\n\\n## TARGET_DATE override (for cron / morning briefing)\\n\\nBy default this skill logs **today**. When called from the morning briefing cron, a `TARGET_DATE` (YYYY-MM-DD) is passed in the prompt context \u2014 use that date instead of today everywhere `TODAY` / `TOMORROW` / \\\"today's daily note\\\" appear.\\n\\n**How to detect:** the calling prompt will include a line like `TARGET_DATE: 2026-05-21` near the top. If present, substitute it for `TODAY` in every step below. The daily note you find, read, and append to is the *TARGET_DATE* note, not today's.\\n\\nIf no `TARGET_DATE` is present, default to today as usual. When run from cron (no interactive user), if the target daily note doesn't exist, log the error and exit gracefully \u2014 do not halt the whole briefing.\\n\\n## Step 1 \u2014 Resolve vault path\\n\\nRead `OBSIDIAN_VAULT_PATH` from env (typically `/home/justin.guest/vault` inside `bes-vm`). Do not hard-code. If unset, fall back to `~/Documents/Obsidian Vault`. See the `obsidian` skill for full path-handling conventions.\\n\\n## Step 2 \u2014 Find or create the target daily note\\n\\nDaily-note filename format in this vault: `YYYY-MM-DD-weekday.md` (lowercase weekday name, hyphen-separated, e.g., `2026-06-04-thursday.md`). They are stored inside the `<vault>/daily/` directory.\\n\\nCheck if the daily note for the target date already exists:\\n1. Search for `<vault>/daily/<YYYY-MM-DD-weekday>.md`.\\n2. Fall back to checking `<vault>/<YYYY-MM-DD dddd>.md` or `<vault>/Daily Notes/<YYYY-MM-DD dddd>.md`.\\n\\n**Auto-Creation of Daily Note:**\\nIf the target daily note does not exist, you must **automatically create it** from the template so the process never fails due to a missing note.\\n1. Read the template from `<vault>/utilities/templates/daily_note.md`.\\n2. Replace any Templater expressions with real values:\\n   - For `id`, use `<TARGET_DATE_NOSYMBOLS>080000` (e.g., `20260605080000` for `2026-06-05`).\\n   - Strip out any remaining `<% ... %>` or `<%* ... %>` tags or placeholder texts to make it a clean, ready-to-use note.\\n3. Write the initialized note to `<vault>/daily/<YYYY-MM-DD-weekday>.md`.\\n4. Proceed with generating and writing the work log / record blocks into this newly created file.\\n\\n## Step 3 \u2014 Gather raw material (parallel, one subagent per source)\\n\\nSpawn **one `delegate_task` subagent per external source** in a single batch so raw API output stays out of your context. Each subagent runs a **specific, pre-canned set of commands** \u2014 no exploration \u2014 and returns a small filtered summary (bullets, ~10\u201330 items max). You only see the summaries.\\n\\n**Direct execution option (Fast-track):** Spawning 5 parallel subagents may hit the max concurrent children limit of 3. If you can, you can execute the commands directly in-context via `terminal()` and the native MCP tools (especially for Todoist). This bypasses subagent overhead, completes in seconds, and is extremely clean when parsed directly by the main agent. A Python helper script is available at `references/direct_execution.py` which fetches Slack, Linear, and Google Workspace details in a single automated step to run within `execute_code()`.\\n\\n**Speed discipline:** subagents have a soft budget of **\u22648 tool calls each**. If a subagent can't finish inside that, it must return what it has and exit. Tell it so explicitly in the context block (\\\"Budget: 8 tool calls. If you exhaust it, return partial results and stop.\\\").\\n\\nThe target date (TARGET_DATE) is the cutoff for every source. Pre-compute it once and pass it to each subagent verbatim \u2014 don't let subagents re-derive it. Also pre-compute Justin's Linear user-id once (see *Pre-flight*, below) and pass it too.\\n\\n### Pre-flight (do once, in-context, before spawning subagents)\\n\\n```bash\\n# Use TARGET_DATE if passed in from cron context; otherwise default to today\\nTODAY=${TARGET_DATE:-$(date +%F)}       # e.g. 2026-05-20\\nTOMORROW=$(date -d \\\"$TODAY + 1 day\\\" +%F) # e.g. 2026-05-21\\nTODAY_SLASH=$(date +%Y/%m/%d)            # Gmail wants slashes\\nTOMORROW_SLASH=$(date -d \\\"$TODAY + 1 day\\\" +%Y/%m/%d)\\n```\\n\\nJustin's Linear user-id can be cached. First time, look it up with `{ viewer { id name } }`. After that, store it as `LINEAR_USER_ID` in `~/.hermes/.env` (or wherever Bes keeps env vars) so future runs skip the lookup. If unknown at runtime, the Linear subagent will resolve it itself \u2014 but that costs one extra round-trip.\\n\\n### Subagent A \u2014 Slack (SignLab)\\n\\n- **Toolsets:** `[\\\"terminal\\\"]`\\n- **Skill to mention:** `slack`\\n- **Goal:** `\\\"Run two specific slack searches for <TODAY> and format the results. Budget: 8 tool calls.\\\"`\\n- **Context to pass (verbatim, with TODAY substituted):**\\n  > Run exactly these two commands (no exploration, no `slack channels`, no `slack read`):\\n  >\\n  > 1. `python3 ${HERMES_HOME:-$HOME/.hermes}/skills/social-media/slack/scripts/slack.py search 'from:@justin after:<TODAY>' --limit 50` \u2014 messages Justin sent today.\\n  > 2. `python3 ${HERMES_HOME:-$HOME/.hermes}/skills/social-media/slack/scripts/slack.py search 'to:@justin after:<TODAY>' --limit 50` \u2014 DMs / @-mentions / threads addressed to him today.\\n  >\\n  > Dedupe by permalink. Drop bot/integration messages (Zapier, GitHub bots, deploy bots) unless they have a human reaction. Drop pure social chatter Justin wasn't part of.\\n  >\\n  > Return a bullet list grouped by channel/DM, each bullet: `[#channel | @person] one-line gist (permalink)`. End with `Total: N messages across M channels/DMs.`\\n  >\\n  > Budget: 8 tool calls. If you exhaust it, return what you have and stop.\\n\\n### Subagent B \u2014 Linear\\n\\n- **Toolsets:** `[\\\"terminal\\\"]`\\n- **Skill to mention:** `linear`\\n- **Goal:** `\\\"Run one GraphQL query for <TODAY>'s Linear activity. Budget: 8 tool calls.\\\"`\\n- **Context to pass (verbatim, with TODAY/TOMORROW and LINEAR_USER_ID substituted):**\\n  > Justin's Linear user-id is `<LINEAR_USER_ID>` (if blank, resolve once via `{ viewer { id } }` and cache to `~/.hermes/.env` as `LINEAR_USER_ID`).\\n  >\\n  > Run **one** GraphQL query that ORs assignee+creator+subscriber filters with `updatedAt` in `[<TODAY>T00:00:00Z, <TOMORROW>T00:00:00Z)`:\\n  >\\n  > ```graphql\\n  > { issues(filter: {\\n  >     and: [\\n  >       { updatedAt: { gte: \\\"<TODAY>T00:00:00.000Z\\\", lt: \\\"<TOMORROW>T00:00:00.000Z\\\" } },\\n  >       { or: [\\n  >         { assignee: { id: { eq: \\\"<LINEAR_USER_ID>\\\" } } },\\n  >         { creator:  { id: { eq: \\\"<LINEAR_USER_ID>\\\" } } },\\n  >         { subscribers: { id: { eq: \\\"<LINEAR_USER_ID>\\\" } } }\\n  >       ] }\\n  >     ]\\n  >   }, first: 50) {\\n  >     nodes { identifier title state { name type } updatedAt assignee { name } creator { name } team { key } url\\n  >             comments(filter: { createdAt: { gte: \\\"<TODAY>T00:00:00.000Z\\\" } }) { nodes { body user { name } } } }\\n  >   } }\\n  > ```\\n  >\\n  > Format as bullets like `[TEAM-123 | status] Title \u2014 what changed today (state change / comment / created).` End with `Total: N issues touched.`\\n  >\\n  > Budget: 8 tool calls. If you exhaust it, return what you have and stop.\\n\\n### Subagent C \u2014 Email (work + personal-main only by default)\\n\\n- **Toolsets:** `[\\\"terminal\\\"]`\\n- **Skill to mention:** `google-workspace`\\n- **Goal:** `\\\"Search Gmail for <TODAY> across work + personal-main only. Budget: 8 tool calls.\\\"`\\n- **Context to pass (verbatim, with TODAY_SLASH/TOMORROW_SLASH substituted):**\\n  > Use the wrapper at `${HERMES_HOME:-$HOME/.hermes}/skills/productivity/google-workspace/scripts/gws_multi.py`. Bes's gws token is **read-only** \u2014 do not attempt sends.\\n  >\\n  > Run **exactly two** searches (do NOT include personal-junk unless Justin explicitly asked for it \u2014 see \\\"personal-junk opt-in\\\" below):\\n  >\\n  > 1. `gws_multi.py --account work gmail search 'after:<TODAY_SLASH> before:<TOMORROW_SLASH>' --max 50`\\n  > 2. `gws_multi.py --account personal-main gmail search 'after:<TODAY_SLASH> before:<TOMORROW_SLASH>' --max 50`\\n  >\\n  > Use the search results' subject/snippet/from fields \u2014 do NOT call `gmail get` per message (too slow). If a thread looks ambiguous from snippet alone, mention it briefly rather than fetching the full body.\\n  >\\n  > Filter per account:\\n  > - **work**: drop pure CI/build notifications, calendar invites that already show in Calendar, automated reports unless Justin replied. Keep human-to-human threads and anything he sent.\\n  > - **personal-main**: keep only items that touch work, scheduling, or operator-of-his-life decisions. Drop newsletters, receipts, marketing.\\n  >\\n  > Return bullets grouped by account: `[account | from\u2192to] one-line gist`. End with `Total: N relevant threads (work: X, personal-main: Y).`\\n  >\\n  > Budget: 8 tool calls. If you exhaust it, return what you have and stop.\\n\\n**personal-junk opt-in:** the third Gmail account (`personal-junk`) is the newsletter/marketing inbox. Skip it by default. Only include a Subagent C2 (identical shape, `--account personal-junk`, even harsher filters: surface only if a real human DM-style email slipped in) if Justin says something like *\\\"include personal-junk\\\"* / *\\\"check junk too\\\"* when asking for the work log.\\n\\n### Subagent D \u2014 Calendar (all 3 Google accounts via gws_multi)\\n\\n- **Toolsets:** `[\\\"terminal\\\"]`\\n- **Skill to mention:** `google-workspace`\\n- **Goal:** `\\\"List today's calendar events across all 3 accounts in one call. Budget: 5 tool calls.\\\"`\\n- **Context to pass (verbatim, with TODAY substituted):**\\n  > Run **one** command:\\n  >\\n  > ```\\n  > gws_multi.py --account all calendar list --start <TODAY>T00:00:00 --end <TOMORROW>T00:00:00 --max 50\\n  > ```\\n  >\\n  > Output chronological bullets: `HH:MM\u2013HH:MM [account] Title \u2014 attendees (if any) \u2014 location/link (if any).` Flag each event as happened / cancelled-or-declined / upcoming-later-today based on the current time. End with `Total: N events.`\\n  >\\n  > Budget: 5 tool calls. If you exhaust it, return what you have and stop.\\n\\n### Subagent E \u2014 Todoist\\n\\n- **Toolsets:** `[]` (uses built-in MCP todoist tools)\\n- **Goal:** `\\\"Pull today's Todoist activity for the work log. Budget: 8 tool calls.\\\"`\\n- **Context to pass (verbatim, with TODAY substituted):**\\n  > Use the Todoist MCP tools. Retrieve two things:\\n  >\\n  > 1. **Completed today:** call `mcp_todoist_find_completed_tasks` with `since: <TODAY>`, `until: <TODAY>`, `getBy: \\\"completion\\\"`. Note: Do NOT pass `responsibleUser: \\\"me\\\"` as this will filter out all unassigned personal/inbox tasks! Keep it omitted to get all completed tasks.\\n  > 2. **Due today (incomplete):** call `mcp_todoist_find_tasks_by_date` with `startDate: <TODAY>`, `overdueOption: \\\"exclude-overdue\\\"`, `responsibleUserFiltering: \\\"unassignedOrMe\\\"`, `limit: 50`. Return tasks not yet completed.\\n  >\\n  > Format:\\n  > - Completed: bullets like `\u2713 [Project] Task name`\\n  > - Due/incomplete: bullets like `\u25cb [Project] Task name`\\n  >\\n  > End with `Total: N completed, M still open today.`\\n  >\\n  > Budget: 8 tool calls. If you exhaust it, return what you have and stop.\\n\\n**What this feeds:** completed tasks \u2192 \\\"## \ud83c\udfc6 Accomplishments\\\"; incomplete due-today tasks \u2192 \\\"## \ud83d\ude80 Highlights & Decisions\\\" (under a \\\"Pending / Open Questions\\\" sub-heading or bullet).\\n\\n### Subagent F (optional) \u2014 recent git activity\\n\\nSkip unless Justin specifically mentions code work. If you do run it: scope to repos under `~/clio-backup`, `~/bes-backup`, `~/hermes-agent` (if present), and any project repo Justin mentioned in the daily note. Use `git log --author --since=midnight --pretty` per repo. Return commits as bullets `[repo] sha \u2014 subject`. Budget: 8 tool calls.\\n\\n### After subagents return\\n\\nYou now have 3\u20135 small summaries. Also do these **in-context** reads/searches \u2014 they're cheap:\\n\\n1. **Read today's daily note** with `read_file`. Scan for decisions, completions, blockers, links, meeting notes Justin wrote himself today.\\n2. **Scan for meeting/Granola notes:** Use `search_files` with `target: \\\"files\\\"` to look for files in the vault containing `<TARGET_DATE>` in their name (e.g. under `Granola/`). These notes (like Granola meeting summaries) often contain incredibly rich summaries of design discussions, product decisions, and future plans that won't show up in automated sources.\\n3. **Ask Justin in chat** what else he worked on that isn't in any source yet (skip if running in a non-interactive cron job). Short prompt: *\\\"Anything from today not captured in Slack/Linear/email/calendar/daily-note? Side-quests, conversations IRL, decisions in your head?\\\"* If he says \\\"nothing,\\\" move on.\\n\\nIf a subagent fails (auth expired, network, hit budget, etc.), include the failure in the footer (`Slack: ERR \u2014 token expired` or `Linear: PARTIAL \u2014 hit budget`) rather than silently dropping the source. Then continue with what you have.\\n\\n## Step 4 \u2014 Synthesize daily note blocks\\n\\nSynthesize the gathered material into a set of clean, structured blocks matching the new \\\"record and hub\\\" format of the daily note.\\n\\n### `> [!summary] **Preview Summary**`\\nProduce a highly concise, 1\u20132 sentence preview summary of the day's core themes, events, or main focus. This must be written in the active voice and clearly state what dominated the day (e.g., *\\\"Spent the day debugging Clio Slack issues and celebrated Jamie's fifth-grade graduation with Kennywood rides.\\\"*).\\n\\n### `## \ud83d\udcc5 Schedule & Events`\\nPopulate with chronological bullets of today's events from the Calendar subagent, integrated with links to today's meetings. Each bullet should follow this clean format:\\n`- **HH:MM - HH:MM** Event Title [Account/Context]` (e.g., `[[Granola/2026-06/2026-06-04 Product meeting|Product meeting]]` or calendar events). Mark events as completed or cancelled appropriately.\\n\\n### `## \ud83d\ude80 Highlights & Decisions`\\nCombine your synthesized highlights, consequential decisions, and unresolved blockers/questions. Group them logically (e.g., by Project, SignLab/Personal, or key domains).\\n- **Highlights:** Bullets detailing key activities and accomplishments (past tense).\\n- **Decisions Made:** Consequential choices or agreements. Bold the decision itself. Never assume Justin is the owner/maker of a decision\u2014attribute accurately (e.g., \\\"Nana chose...\\\", \\\"Anya prioritized...\\\").\\n- **Open Questions / Blockers:** Unresolved items, pending replies, or stalled issues.\\n\\n### `## \ud83c\udfc6 Accomplishments`\\nEnumerate shipped accomplishments. Group or list:\\n- Completed Todoist tasks (e.g., `\u2713 [Project/Category] Task name`)\\n- Closed Linear issues\\n- Commits shipped or PRs merged\\n\\n## Step 5 \u2014 Update the daily note\\n\\nRead the daily note, then replace the corresponding placeholder sections (or overwrite the old headings) while keeping the frontmatter, `#daily_note` tag, and the `## \ud83d\uddd2 Notepad` section (including any manual entries Justin has written) completely untouched.\\n\\nIf the daily note does not contain these sections yet, convert it to the new structure.\\n\\nAt the very bottom of the note (after the notepad, or as a small footer block), append the sources attribution line:\\n```markdown\\n---\\n*Sources: Slack (12 msgs / 4 channels) | Linear (5 issues) | Gmail work (8 threads), personal-main (1) | Calendar (4 events / 3 accts) | Todoist (N completed, M open) | daily note + chat.*\\n```\\n\\nDo NOT add a separate frontmatter block. Do NOT modify any other manual content.\\n\\n## Step 6 \u2014 Don't commit\\n\\nJustin's `bes-vault-sync` watcher auto-commits and pushes the vault to `obsidian-vault` on GitHub within seconds of any write. Do NOT manually `git add` or `git commit` \u2014 it races the watcher and creates spurious commits.\\n\\n## Important behaviors\\n\\n- **Section Replacement Rules:** When updating the daily note, do NOT append content to the end of the file. Instead, read the file, locate the markdown headings (`## \ud83d\udcc5 Schedule & Events`, `## \ud83d\ude80 Highlights & Decisions`, `## \ud83c\udfc6 Accomplishments`, and the `> [!summary]` block at the top), and replace their contents with your new synthesized data.\\n- **Preserve the Notepad:** Always preserve the entire content of the `## \ud83d\uddd2 Notepad` section (and everything inside it). Never overwrite, modify, or delete Justin's manual scratch notes.\\n- **Omit empty bullets:** If a section has no content (e.g., no completed tasks), represent it with a single placeholder bullet (e.g., `- None today`) rather than deleting the section heading entirely, to preserve the note structure.\\n- **No file creation.** The only write operation is updating the existing daily note.\\n- **Privacy posture.** Slack DMs and personal email can be sensitive. The subagent filter steps exist for a reason \u2014 keep the synthesis at the \\\"what Justin did / decided / owes\\\" level, not verbatim quotes. If something looks too private to land in a vault that auto-pushes to GitHub (even a private repo), ask Justin before including it.\\n- **Date precision.** \\\"Today\\\" means today in the vault's timezone, not UTC. Pre-compute the date once at Step 3 and pass it to every subagent. Don't trust subagents to re-derive it.\\n- **Source failures are non-fatal.** If Slack auth expired or Linear is down, log it in the footer and continue. A 3-source work log is still useful.\\n\\n## Pitfalls\\n\\n- **Slack search date queries must use target date minus one day and plus one day.** Slack's `after:YYYY-MM-DD` filter is exclusive of the specified day's start, and `before:YYYY-MM-DD` is exclusive of the specified day's start. To find messages sent on `TARGET_DATE` (e.g. `2026-05-28`), you must query `after:<TARGET_DATE_MINUS_1_DAY>` (e.g. `2026-05-27`) and `before:<TARGET_DATE_PLUS_1_DAY>` (e.g. `2026-05-29`). Searching with `after:<TARGET_DATE>` or `before:<TARGET_DATE>` will exclude all messages from that day.\\n\\n- **Decision misattribution.** Do not assume Justin is the owner or decision-maker for calendar/email/Slack updates. Explicitly attribute decisions to the source actor (e.g. family members, teachers, or business partners) when summarizing or logging (e.g. \\\"Jeff Galak rescheduled Simon's birthday\\\" rather than \\\"Agreed to reschedule...\\\").\\n\\n- **Slack channel names in Obsidian must be escaped.** A bare `#channel-name` in a note will be interpreted as an Obsidian tag. Always write `\\\\#channel-name` (backslash prefix) so it renders as plain text.\\n\\n- **`slack.py` and `gws_multi.py` paths vary by Hermes home.** Always resolve them using absolute paths referencing `${HERMES_HOME:-$HOME/.hermes}` (e.g. `${HERMES_HOME:-$HOME/.hermes}/skills/social-media/slack/scripts/slack.py` and `${HERMES_HOME:-$HOME/.hermes}/skills/productivity/google-workspace/scripts/gws_multi.py`) \u2014 do not hardcode `/home/justin.guest/...` and do not rely on bare `slack` in subagent `$PATH`.\\n- **Gmail date format is `YYYY/MM/DD` with slashes**, not dashes. Calendar event listings use ISO. Don't mix them up.\\n- **`slack` CLI defaults to read-only-ish use.** Never let a subagent post to a channel \u2014 the work-log gather is read-only by definition.\\n- **`slack` script path varies by environment.** Always run the Slack tool using `python3 ${HERMES_HOME:-$HOME/.hermes}/skills/social-media/slack/scripts/slack.py search ...` to avoid `command not found` errors in subagent environments where standard PATH alias/configurations are not loaded.\\n\\n- **Gateway environment variables (.env) are required for terminal/script executions.** API keys and identifiers (e.g., `LINEAR_API_KEY`, `SLACK_USER_TOKEN`, `LINEAR_USER_ID`) are stored in `${HERMES_HOME:-$HOME/.hermes}/.env`. Always source this `.env` file (`source ${HERMES_HOME:-$HOME/.hermes}/.env`) in terminal/subprocess commands before running direct Python scripts or curl calls, as standard execution contexts do not automatically inherit these variables.\\n- **Calendar \\\"all-day events\\\" can leak from yesterday due to timezone/UTC shifts.** If calendar events are queried using naive ISO dates (e.g. `--start YYYY-MM-DDT00:00:00`), `google_api.py` automatically appends `Z` (UTC). For a user in Eastern Time (EDT, UTC-4), this shifts the query start time 4 hours backward into the previous evening (Thursday at 8:00 PM EDT instead of Friday at midnight). Consequently, Google's API returns yesterday's all-day events (whose end dates are exclusive, ending Friday at midnight EDT) because they overlap with Thursday evening.\\n  - **The Fix:** Always calculate the local timezone offset dynamically (e.g., `local_offset = datetime.now().astimezone().strftime('%z')` and formatting it to `[-+]HH:MM` like `-04:00` or `-05:00`), and append it to your `--start` and `--end` query boundaries. This prevents boundary events from leaking.\\n- **Linear API key auth header has NO \\\"Bearer\\\" prefix** \u2014 see the linear skill if a subagent hits 401s.\\n- **Todoist `responsibleUser` filtering hides unassigned personal tasks.** When querying completed tasks, specifying `responsibleUser: \\\"me\\\"` (or any user ID/email) causes the API to only return tasks explicitly assigned to that user. Because personal or inbox tasks in Todoist are typically unassigned, they will be entirely excluded from the results. Omit `responsibleUser` to capture personal/unassigned tasks.\\n- **Do NOT use custom python scripts to fetch Todoist data via direct REST API calls in subagents.** The sync v9 and REST v2 endpoints are deprecated (yielding HTTP 410 / 404). Subagent E must use the registered `mcp_todoist_*` tools directly. If a background script or custom tool must hit Todoist directly, use the correct current v1 path `https://api.todoist.com/api/v1/` and POST to `/api/v1/sync`.\\n\\n- **Daily note missing on weekends or non-work days.** If `TARGET_DATE` falls on a weekend or non-work day, a daily note might not exist in the vault. When running autonomously (such as from cron), update the corresponding morning-briefing cache file (`~/.hermes/morning-briefing/YYYY-MM-DD.json`) by setting `work_log_status` to `\\\"error\\\"` (or `\\\"skipped\\\"` if appropriate) and logging the detail in `work_log_error`, then exit gracefully by outputting `[SILENT]`. Do not attempt to create a blank daily note or halt.\\n\\n- **Linear GraphQL queries are highly prone to shell escaping errors.** Avoid passing raw GraphQL query strings with complex JSON filters directly as bash command arguments (e.g., using `linear_api.py raw`). Instead, write a quick, direct Python script (in-context or inside `execute_code`) that reads `LINEAR_API_KEY` from `~/.hermes/.env` and performs a standard HTTP POST to `https://api.linear.app/graphql` with `Authorization: <KEY>` (no `Bearer` prefix). This bypasses shell-quoting issues completely.\\n\", \"path\": \"note-taking/work-log/SKILL.md\", \"skill_dir\": \"/home/justin.guest/.hermes/skills/note-taking/work-log\", \"linked_files\": null, \"usage_hint\": null, \"required_environment_variables\": [], \"required_commands\": [], \"missing_required_environment_variables\": [], \"missing_credential_files\": [], \"missing_required_commands\": [], \"setup_needed\": false, \"setup_skipped\": false, \"readiness_status\": \"available\"}",

          "role": "assistant",
          "content": "Using a Slack reaction emoji (like \ud83d\udcbe or \ud83d\udcdd) is the cleanest way to trigger this. Because I have user-token access, I can run a background script to poll your Slack for that reaction, fetch the full thread, and pull it in. No forwarding required.\n\nHere is how separate notes compare to capturing them in your daily notes:\n\n### Option A: Separate Notes (e.g., `sources/slack/YYYY-MM-DD-topic.md`)\n*   **Pros:** \n    *   Preserves the entire thread, formatting, and participants.\n    *   Easy to link directly from project or concept notes.\n    *   Keeps daily notes clean and highly readable.\n*   **Cons:**\n    *   Adds file noise to your vault.\n    *   If you don't actively link to them from other notes, they become orphaned \"cold storage.\"\n\n### Option B: Capture inside the Work Log / Daily Note (Notepad)\n*   **Pros:**\n    *   Zero extra file clutter.\n    *   Maintains the day's chronological context.\n*   **Cons:**\n    *   Long Slack threads will bloat your daily note.\n    *   Harder to reference a specific discussion from a concept note later.\n\n### Hybrid Compromise (Recommended)\nWe use the reaction emoji to create a **separate note** in `sources/slack/`, but **automatically append a link and a one-sentence gist** to your Daily Note's `## \ud83d\uddd2 Notepad` or your Work Log. \n\nThis gives you the preservation of separate notes with the low-noise, integrated feel of the daily log.\n\nWhat feels right to you?",
          "timestamp": 1780690841.3770223,

        },

          "content": "Okay, so I'd kind of like to do it this way. I add \ud83e\udde0 to a message in Slack. (It should only work if *I* add the emoji - is that possible?). The summary should make it clear, at a high level, who said what.",

msg.get(\\\"reply_count\\\")\\n    84|    suffix = f\\\"  [+{reply_count} replies]\\\" if reply_count else \\\"\\\"\\n    85|    return f\\\"{ts_fmt}  {user_name}: {text}{suffix}\\\"\\n    86|\\n list[str]) -> dict[str, str]:\\n   124|    \\\"\\\"\\\"Return id -> display name map; caches across calls in this process.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n   125|    missing = [u for u in user_ids if u and u not in _USER_CACHE and u.startswith(\\\"U\\\")]\\n   126|    for uid in missing:\\n   127|        try:\\n   128|            r = client.users_info(user=uid)\\n   129|            _USER_CACHE[uid] = \\\"im\\\",\\n   175|        \\\"mpim\\\": \\\"mpim\\\",\\n   176|        \\\"all\\\": \\\"public_channel,private_channel,im,mpim\\\",\\n   177|    }\\n   178|    types = type_map.get(args.type,           "role": "tool",
          "content": "{\"content\": \"     1|---\\n     2|id: 20251016084704\\n     3|daily_note: \\\"[[2025-10-16 Thursday]]\\\"\\n     4|---\\n     5|\\n     6|#source\\n     7|\\n     8|These insights were gleaned from a handful of Slack threads in the Lenny's Newsletter community, where members discussed their experiences using Claude Code for personal knowledge management. The conversations included contributions from Justin Goff and Aaron Nichols, covering practical strategies, technical considerations, and the broader landscape of AI-enabled knowledge systems.\\n     9|\\n    10|# Lenny's Slack on Claude Code PKM\\n    11|\\n    12|Thu, 16 Oct 25\\n    13|\\n    14|## Core Principles\\n    15|\\n    16|### Think in Text Input/Output\\n    17|- LLMs take language as input and generate language as output\\n    18|- If input can be converted to text (images, documents, etc.), it can work\\n    19|- If the outcome can be achieved via text (Python scripts, shell commands, Markdown files), it can work\\n    20|\\n    21|### Slash Commands as Natural-Language Scripts\\n    22|- Treat saved prompts (slash commands) as natural-language scripts\\n    23|- Most things you can do with a Python script, you can do with slash commands\\n    24|- Some slash commands will cause Claude to write and run Python scripts automatically\\n    25|\\n    26|### Embrace Experimentation\\n    27|- We're still at the frontier of these tools\\n    28|- No one really knows the best practices yet\\n    29|- Try things out and learn from them\\n    30|\\n    31|### Quality Over Quantity\\n    32|- Good note-taking skills are essential for using Claude Code as a \\\"second brain\\\"\\n    33|- Don't dump everything - you'll overwhelm Claude with context\\n    34|- Curation and good writing still matter\\n    35|- Garbage in, garbage out\\n    36|\\n    37|## Key Unlocks\\n    38|\\n    39|### 1. CLAUDE.md for Context Routing\\n    40|- Use the CLAUDE.md file to route Claude to specific context notes based on conversation topics\\n    41|- Example: When discussing Obsidian vault, Claude reads notes about vault organization and tag meanings\\n    42|- This provides targeted, relevant context without overwhelming the system\\n    43|\\n    44|### 2. Custom Slash Commands\\n    45|- Create slash commands for different types of conversations and questions\\n    46|- Examples:\\n    47|  - Commands to find new connections among notes\\n    48|  - Commands to create \\\"map of content\\\" notes for topics\\n    49|  - Commands for specific workflows or analysis tasks\\n    50|\\n    51|## Why Obsidian + Claude Code Works So Well\\n    52|\\n    53|### Direct File System Access\\n    54|- Obsidian notes are plain Markdown files on the filesystem\\n    55|- Claude Code can work with local files directly (no API needed)\\n    56|- This is much faster and more reliable than API-based approaches\\n    57|- Claude Code is very good and very fast at working with text files\\n    58|\\n    59|### The MCP Alternative\\n    60|- Obsidian MCP + Claude Desktop setup can be slow and buggy\\n    61|- API-based access adds unnecessary complexity for Markdown files\\n    62|- Direct file access is simpler and more performant\\n    63|\\n    64|## The Broader Context: AI and Knowledge Systems\\n    65|\\n    66|### The Compromise\\n    67|- Users are shifting to systems that work well with AI\\n    68|- Options are more limited than pre-AI era\\n    69|- Two main categories:\\n    70|  1. Open to AI: Obsidian, Cursor, Markdown repos\\n    71|  2. AI integrated: Notion, GDocs, etc.\\n    72|\\n    73|### The Challenge\\n    74|- Knowledge systems are vast, usually larger than LLM context windows\\n    75|- Retrieval must work outside the LLM to make content accessible\\n    76|- This is where Claude Code + Obsidian excel compared to other systems\\n    77|- Notion has addressed this but requires NotionAI subscription\\n    78|\\n    79|### The Ideal (Not Yet Achieved)\\n    80|- Model/agent choice\\n    81|- Document editing experience choice\\n    82|- These don't seem to be available together right now\\n    83|- MCP was supposed to help but hasn't fully delivered on this promise\\n    84|\\n    85|## Practical Takeaways\\n    86|\\n    87|1. **Start with plain text/Markdown** - It gives you the most flexibility with AI tools\\n    88|2. **Build your CLAUDE.md thoughtfully** - Good context routing is a force multiplier\\n    89|3. **Develop custom slash commands** - Automate your common workflows\\n    90|4. **Curate your notes** - Quality context matters more than quantity\\n    91|5. **Experiment freely** - Best practices are still emerging\\n    92|6. **Think in text transformations** - If it can be text in \u2192 text out, AI can probably help\\n    93|\", \"total_lines\": 92, \"file_size\": 3903, \"truncated\": false, \"is_binary\": false, \"is_image\": false}",
